data.title: Craft at Home
data.hero_headline: Your kitchen is the best cafe in town.
data.hero_subtext: When making coffee at home becomes something closer to a practice than a habit.
data.editorial_intro: There is a point where home coffee stops being a morning routine and becomes a craft. You weigh your doses. You track your extractions. You have opinions about water chemistry. This is not a phase — it is who you are now. And your equipment should re
data.excerpt: When making coffee at home becomes something closer to a practice than a habit.

data.title: Espresso Anywhere
data.hero_headline: Hotel coffee is not coffee. You solved that.
data.hero_subtext: Because the worst part of travelling should never be the espresso.
data.editorial_intro: You have stood in enough hotel rooms staring at a Nespresso machine with stale capsules to know that you cannot leave this to chance anymore. You travel for work, for pleasure, for life — and you refuse to spend a single morning drinking something th
data.excerpt: Because the worst part of travelling should never be the espresso.

data.title: The Morning Minimalist
data.hero_headline: One cup. No compromise.
data.hero_subtext: For the person who believes that fewer things, done well, is the whole point.
data.editorial_intro: You don't need a shelf of gadgets or a complicated routine. You need one excellent cup, made quietly and precisely, before the rest of the day begins. The morning minimalist understands that quality isn't about accumulation — it's about distillation.
data.excerpt: For the person who believes that fewer things, done well, is the whole point.

data.title: The Non-Barista
data.hero_headline: Great coffee. Zero learning curve.
data.hero_subtext: You want the result, not the hobby. That's perfectly valid.
data.editorial_intro: Not everyone wants to weigh beans, time shots, and debate extraction theory. Some people want excellent coffee that appears when they press a button. You are one of those people, and there is absolutely nothing wrong with that. The Arco Automatico wa
data.excerpt: You want the result, not the hobby.

data.title: The Upgrade Path
data.hero_headline: You've outgrown your setup. You know it.
data.hero_subtext: When the beans are better than the machine, it's time.
data.editorial_intro: You have been drinking good coffee for years. You have tried the specialty roasters, dialled in your grind, watched the videos, read the forums. And every morning, you taste the gap between what your beans could be and what your current setup actuall
data.excerpt: When the beans are better than the machine, it's time.

data.title: Italian Espresso Culture
data.hero_headline: Stand at the bar. Drink it in three sips. This is where it all began.
data.hero_subtext: Arco was born in this tradition. Every machine we make carries it forward.
data.editorial_intro: In Italy, espresso is not a drink you order and carry to a table. It is a civic ritual performed standing at a bar, completed in under ninety seconds, and repeated two or three times a day by virtually every adult in the country. It is the connective
data.excerpt: Arco was born in this tradition.

data.title: Precision Coffee Germany
data.hero_headline: Measured. Calibrated. Repeatable. Coffee as an engineering discipline.
data.hero_subtext: In Germany, the engineering mindset meets the brewing process — and the results are extraordinary.
data.editorial_intro: Germany is a country that builds cars to tolerances measured in microns, that engineers bridges to last centuries, and that approaches every technical challenge with a rigour that the rest of the world admires and occasionally finds intimidating. It
data.excerpt: In Germany, the engineering mindset meets the brewing process — and the results are extraordinary.

data.title: Scandinavian Filter Tradition
data.hero_headline: Light roasts. High extraction. The Nordic way of coffee.
data.hero_subtext: In Scandinavia, coffee is brewed lighter, cleaner, and more intentionally than anywhere else in the world.
data.editorial_intro: Scandinavian coffee culture is the inverse of Italian espresso tradition — where Italy goes dark, Nordic countries go light. Where Italy favours intensity and brevity, Scandinavia favours clarity and volume. The Nordic approach to coffee has quietly
data.excerpt: In Scandinavia, coffee is brewed lighter, cleaner, and more intentionally than anywhere else in the world.

data.title: Third Wave UK
data.hero_headline: London. Manchester. Edinburgh. The UK's specialty coffee revolution is here.
data.hero_subtext: From flat whites to single-origin espressos, British coffee culture has transformed in a decade — and the home setup has kept pace.
data.editorial_intro: Ten years ago, specialty coffee in the UK was a niche interest pursued by a small community of obsessives in east London warehouses. Today, every major British city has a thriving scene of independent roasters, specialty cafes, and a generation of dr
data.excerpt: From flat whites to single-origin espressos, British coffee culture has transformed in a decade — and the home setup has kept pace.

data.title: Dedicated Coffee Corner
data.hero_headline: You built it a corner. It deserved one.
data.hero_subtext: When the coffee setup stops sharing counter space and gets its own dedicated station.
data.editorial_intro: It started with the machine on the main counter, squeezed between the toaster and the fruit bowl. Then the grinder arrived and needed its own spot. Then the accessories. At some point you looked at the kitchen and realised that coffee had colonised a
data.excerpt: When the coffee setup stops sharing counter space and gets its own dedicated station.

data.title: Home Office Setup
data.hero_headline: The cafe between your desk and your kitchen.
data.hero_subtext: When home became the office, the commute became the walk to the coffee machine.
data.editorial_intro: Your commute is fourteen steps. From the desk in the spare bedroom to the coffee station in the hallway, and back again. It is the shortest commute you have ever had, but it might be the most important, because those fourteen steps are the boundary b
data.excerpt: When home became the office, the commute became the walk to the coffee machine.

data.title: Open-Plan Kitchen
data.hero_headline: When the kitchen is the living room, everything has to look right and sound right.
data.hero_subtext: Aesthetics and acoustics matter when there are no walls to hide behind.
data.editorial_intro: In an open-plan home, the kitchen is not a separate room — it is the centre of everything. Cooking, eating, working, relaxing, all of it happens within earshot and line of sight. Your coffee equipment is not tucked away behind a door. It is part of t
data.excerpt: Aesthetics and acoustics matter when there are no walls to hide behind.

data.title: Rented Apartment
data.hero_headline: No plumbing. No mods. No landlord negotiations.
data.hero_subtext: A proper espresso setup that moves when you do.
data.editorial_intro: You rent. This means the kitchen is not yours to modify, the plumbing is not yours to tap into, and any hole you drill in the wall will cost you a portion of the deposit. Your coffee setup needs to work within these constraints — self-contained, port
data.excerpt: A proper espresso setup that moves when you do.

data.title: Small Kitchen Setup
data.hero_headline: Under 40 centimetres. Every millimetre earns its place.
data.hero_subtext: Great coffee in small spaces is an engineering problem. Here is the solution.
data.editorial_intro: Your kitchen was not designed for a coffee setup. It was barely designed for cooking. The counter is shallow, the worktop is short, and every appliance competes for the same thirty-eight centimetres of depth. But you refuse to accept that limited spa
data.excerpt: Great coffee in small spaces is an engineering problem.

data.title: Back From Naples
data.hero_headline: You stood at a bar in Naples. Now you cannot go back.
data.hero_subtext: The holiday ended. The standard for coffee did not.
data.editorial_intro: It was a Tuesday afternoon in the Quartieri Spagnoli. You walked into a bar with no name on the door, stood at the zinc counter with the locals, and a man in a white shirt placed a small cup in front of you without asking what you wanted. One sip, an
data.excerpt: The holiday ended.

data.title: First Apartment
data.hero_headline: Your first kitchen. Your first real coffee setup.
data.hero_subtext: The machine that makes a new place feel like home before the furniture arrives.
data.editorial_intro: The boxes are still stacked in the living room. The Wi-Fi is not set up yet. You ate dinner last night sitting on the floor with a fork and a takeaway container. But the coffee machine — that was the first thing unpacked. Because a place with good co
data.excerpt: The machine that makes a new place feel like home before the furniture arrives.

data.title: Gifting for a Coffee Lover
data.hero_headline: They are impossible to buy for. Until now.
data.hero_subtext: A guide to gifting for the person who already has opinions about water temperature.
data.editorial_intro: You love someone who loves coffee. This should make gift-giving easy, but it does the opposite, because they already own a machine, already have a grinder, and already have strong, specific opinions about every piece of equipment they use. Buying the
data.excerpt: A guide to gifting for the person who already has opinions about water temperature.

data.title: New Parent Upgrade
data.hero_headline: Less sleep. Less time. The coffee has to be better, not harder.
data.hero_subtext: Parenthood changed everything. Your coffee needs to keep up.
data.editorial_intro: You used to have a morning routine. It involved leisurely grinding, careful tamping, a slow extraction watched with the attention of someone who had nowhere to be. Then the baby arrived, and that routine detonated. Now your mornings are dictated by a
data.excerpt: Parenthood changed everything.

data.title: Remote Work Upgrade
data.hero_headline: The office closed. Your coffee upgrade began.
data.hero_subtext: When 'working from home temporarily' became 'this is just how it works now.'
data.editorial_intro: For the first few months, you told yourself it was temporary. The laptop on the kitchen table, the video calls from the sofa, the instant coffee because the office machine was no longer available. Then the email arrived confirming the permanent remot
data.excerpt: When 'working from home temporarily' became 'this is just how it works now.'

data.title: Retirement: Time to Learn
data.hero_headline: Forty years of drinking coffee. Now, finally, time to understand it.
data.hero_subtext: Retirement is not slowing down. It is finally having the time to do things properly.
data.editorial_intro: For decades, coffee was functional. It appeared in your cup at the office, at restaurants, from machines in airport lounges. You drank it constantly and thought about it rarely. Now the working life is over, the diary is empty, and you have discovere
data.excerpt: Retirement is not slowing down.

data.title: Commuter One Cup
data.hero_headline: One cup between the alarm and the 7:42.
data.hero_subtext: The train will not wait. Your coffee should not make you late.
data.editorial_intro: Your morning runs on a schedule that is not yours. The train leaves at 7:42. The walk to the station takes eleven minutes. Breakfast, shower, dressing — all of it is reverse-engineered from that departure time. The coffee has to fit inside the gaps,
data.excerpt: The train will not wait.

data.title: Family Kitchen Morning
data.hero_headline: Four people. Four preferences. One kitchen.
data.hero_subtext: When the morning coffee run serves an espresso, a latte, a hot chocolate, and a long filter — all before 8am.
data.editorial_intro: The family kitchen at 7:15am is a study in controlled chaos. Someone needs a packed lunch. Someone cannot find their shoes. And four different people want four different drinks. You have learned to navigate this with a system that looks effortless bu
data.excerpt: When the morning coffee run serves an espresso, a latte, a hot chocolate, and a long filter — all before 8am.

data.title: Pre-Gym Espresso
data.hero_headline: 5:45am. One shot. Out the door.
data.hero_subtext: The fastest good espresso you can make, for the morning that starts before the morning.
data.editorial_intro: The alarm goes off in the dark. You do not snooze it. Your bag is packed from the night before, your clothes are laid out, and the only thing standing between you and the gym is one espresso. You need it fast, you need it strong, and you need it to b
data.excerpt: The fastest good espresso you can make, for the morning that starts before the morning.

data.title: Slow Weekend Morning
data.hero_headline: Saturday morning. No alarm. No rush.
data.hero_subtext: The coffee you make when time is the only luxury that matters.
data.editorial_intro: There is no alarm on Saturday. You wake when you wake, and the morning stretches ahead of you with nothing in it but potential. This is when coffee becomes something more than fuel. It becomes the centrepiece of the only hours in the week that belong
data.excerpt: The coffee you make when time is the only luxury that matters.

data.title: Work From Home Morning
data.hero_headline: The mid-morning cup that rescues the whole day.
data.hero_subtext: Between meetings, between tasks, between the first coffee and lunch — this is the one that counts.
data.editorial_intro: The first coffee of the day happened at 7am, mechanical and half-conscious. It did its job. But now it is 10:30, the morning meetings have blurred together, and you need something that is not just caffeine but a genuine interruption — a three-minute
data.excerpt: Between meetings, between tasks, between the first coffee and lunch — this is the one that counts.

data.title: Autumn Comfort Drinks
data.hero_headline: The season changes. Your coffee gets richer.
data.hero_subtext: Autumn espresso is bolder, warmer, and more generous. Like the season itself.
data.editorial_intro: There is a moment in early October when the air turns and everything shifts. The light goes golden. The mornings get that particular chill that is not yet cold but is no longer warm. And your coffee, as if responding to the same signal, starts wantin
data.excerpt: Autumn espresso is bolder, warmer, and more generous.

data.title: Autumn Slow Brew
data.hero_headline: The leaves turn. The roasts darken. The mornings slow down.
data.hero_subtext: Autumn is the season that reminds you why you fell in love with coffee in the first place.
data.editorial_intro: There is a week in October when something shifts. The light changes colour, the air has a bite, and the bright, fruity espressos of summer suddenly feel wrong. You reach for the darker roasts, the longer extractions, the richer cups. Autumn is coffee
data.excerpt: Autumn is the season that reminds you why you fell in love with coffee in the first place.

data.title: Back to School: Dorm Coffee Done Right
data.hero_headline: Small room. Small budget. No compromises on the coffee.
data.hero_subtext: The setup that fits on a dorm desk and outlasts your degree.
data.editorial_intro: University coffee is a crisis that nobody talks about. The campus cafe charges four pounds for something that tastes like it was brewed during the previous administration. The instant coffee in the communal kitchen is an insult to the concept of beve
data.excerpt: The setup that fits on a dorm desk and outlasts your degree.

data.title: Christmas Morning Coffee
data.hero_headline: Before the presents. Before the chaos. The coffee.
data.hero_subtext: Christmas morning starts at the machine, and the whole family gathers around it.
data.editorial_intro: Christmas morning is controlled chaos. The children are awake at an hour that should not exist. Wrapping paper is already on the floor. Someone needs batteries. The turkey has a schedule that nobody fully understands. And in the middle of all of it,
data.excerpt: Christmas morning starts at the machine, and the whole family gathers around it.

data.title: Father's Day: His First Real Machine
data.hero_headline: He has been drinking bad coffee for thirty years. Time's up.
data.hero_subtext: The Father's Day gift that changes his mornings for good.
data.editorial_intro: Your father drinks coffee every day. He has done this for decades. And for decades, the coffee has been — at best — adequate. A drip machine that gurgles to life at six, producing a pot of brown liquid that he drinks out of habit rather than pleasure
data.excerpt: The Father's Day gift that changes his mornings for good.

data.title: Holiday Gift Guide Experience
data.hero_headline: The gift that starts every morning right.
data.hero_subtext: Whether you are giving or receiving, Arco makes the holidays taste better.
data.editorial_intro: The holidays bring a particular kind of pressure. You want to give something meaningful, something that will not end up in a drawer by February. And if you are on the receiving end, you want the people who love you to understand that coffee is not ju
data.excerpt: Whether you are giving or receiving, Arco makes the holidays taste better.

data.title: Holiday Gift Guide
data.hero_headline: The gift that starts every morning right — matched to the person who matters.
data.hero_subtext: Persona-matched Christmas gifts for every kind of coffee drinker in your life.
data.editorial_intro: The holidays are approaching and you need gifts for people who love coffee. The problem is that they all love it differently. Your mother wants convenience. Your brother wants craft. Your best friend wants something beautiful on the counter. This is
data.excerpt: Persona-matched Christmas gifts for every kind of coffee drinker in your life.

data.title: New Year Upgrade
data.hero_headline: New year. New setup. The resolution that actually sticks.
data.hero_subtext: January is for upgrading the things that matter most — starting with the first thing you do every morning.
data.editorial_intro: January has a clarity that the rest of the year lacks. The holiday indulgence is over, the new calendar is blank, and there is a particular appetite for improvement that only the first week of the year provides. Most resolutions fail by February. Thi
data.excerpt: January is for upgrading the things that matter most — starting with the first thing you do every morning.

data.title: Spring Single Origins
data.hero_headline: New harvest. New flavours. Your favourite time of year.
data.hero_subtext: Spring means fresh crop arrivals, and the best single origins of the year are landing now.
data.editorial_intro: Every spring, the coffee calendar resets. Fresh harvests arrive from East Africa, Central America, and beyond, and for a few weeks the specialty shelves are stocked with beans at their absolute peak — vibrant, complex, and unlike anything you tasted
data.excerpt: Spring means fresh crop arrivals, and the best single origins of the year are landing now.

data.title: Summer Garden Party Drinks
data.hero_headline: The garden party has a new signature serve.
data.hero_subtext: Shakerato, Espresso Tonic, Iced Americano — summer entertaining, powered by espresso.
data.editorial_intro: The summer garden party has a drinks problem. Wine is expected. Beer is easy. A gin and tonic is reliable. But the coffee option — if there is one — is usually an afterthought: a lukewarm pot of drip coffee sitting on the kitchen counter, ignored by
data.excerpt: Shakerato, Espresso Tonic, Iced Americano — summer entertaining, powered by espresso.

data.title: Summer Iced Espresso
data.hero_headline: When the heat arrives, the coffee adapts.
data.hero_subtext: Cold brew, iced espresso, espresso tonic — summer has its own coffee vocabulary.
data.editorial_intro: June arrives and the hot espresso that anchored your mornings for nine months starts to feel wrong. The kitchen is warm, the cup warms your hands when your hands do not need warming, and the first sip sits heavy in the heat. Summer demands a differen
data.excerpt: Cold brew, iced espresso, espresso tonic — summer has its own coffee vocabulary.

data.title: Valentine's Espresso for Two
data.hero_headline: Love is a double shot, pulled for someone else.
data.hero_subtext: The most romantic gesture is not a reservation. It is making something beautiful before they wake up.
data.editorial_intro: Valentine's Day has been colonized by restaurants, florists, and greeting card companies. The grand gesture — the prix fixe dinner, the dozen roses, the overpriced champagne — is expected, performed, and forgotten by the following Tuesday. The small
data.excerpt: The most romantic gesture is not a reservation.

data.title: Winter Long Brews
data.hero_headline: Short days. Long brews. The season that belongs to filter coffee.
data.hero_subtext: When the mornings are dark and cold, the slow pour-over becomes a meditation.
data.editorial_intro: Winter changes everything about how you drink coffee. The quick espresso that powers your summer mornings feels insufficient when the kitchen is cold and the sky is still dark at eight o'clock. You want something larger, something you can hold in bot
data.excerpt: When the mornings are dark and cold, the slow pour-over becomes a meditation.

data.title: Winter Warmers
data.hero_headline: The cold outside makes the warmth inside matter more.
data.hero_subtext: Winter coffee is slower, richer, and more deliberate. Lean into it.
data.editorial_intro: Something changes about coffee in winter. Not the beans, not the machine — you. The days are short, the mornings are dark, and the cup in your hands becomes more than caffeine. It becomes warmth, ritual, a small act of comfort in a season that demand
data.excerpt: Winter coffee is slower, richer, and more deliberate.

data.title: Clients Coming to Home Office
data.hero_headline: When clients visit, the coffee says as much as the portfolio.
data.hero_subtext: A professional impression, made one cup at a time.
data.editorial_intro: You work from home, and sometimes that means clients come to your home. The spare room is the studio, the dining table is the meeting space, and the impression you make in the first five minutes — including the coffee you serve — sets the tone for th
data.excerpt: A professional impression, made one cup at a time.

data.title: Coffee and Conversation
data.hero_headline: Two cups. One conversation. No rush.
data.hero_subtext: The art of making coffee the reason people sit down and stay.
data.editorial_intro: There is a version of hospitality that has nothing to do with dinner parties or elaborate entertaining. It is simpler than that. Someone comes to your door — a friend, a neighbour, a relative who was passing through — and you say, 'Let me make you a
data.excerpt: The art of making coffee the reason people sit down and stay.

data.title: Coffee for a Dinner Party
data.hero_headline: Eight guests. Eight espressos. The finale the dinner deserves.
data.hero_subtext: The course nobody lists on the menu but everyone remembers.
data.editorial_intro: The meal is over. The conversation has reached that warm, expansive register that only emerges after three courses and two bottles of wine. Someone suggests moving to the sofa. Someone else mentions they should probably call a taxi. And then you say
data.excerpt: The course nobody lists on the menu but everyone remembers.

data.title: Couples Morning Ritual
data.hero_headline: Two people. Two preferences. One machine.
data.hero_subtext: The morning ritual that works for both of you, without compromise.
data.editorial_intro: You drink a straight double espresso, no sugar, no milk. Your partner wants a large oat milk latte with a little foam on top. These are not the same drink. They are barely the same category. And yet, every morning, the same machine makes both of you
data.excerpt: The morning ritual that works for both of you, without compromise.

data.title: Dinner Party Espresso
data.hero_headline: The final course nobody forgets.
data.hero_subtext: When the meal ends and the espresso begins, you own the room.
data.editorial_intro: Dinner parties have acts. The first course sets the tone. The main builds momentum. Dessert draws applause. But the real finale — the one that keeps everyone at the table an extra hour — is espresso. Not coffee from a filter jug. Not a pod. Real espr
data.excerpt: When the meal ends and the espresso begins, you own the room.

data.title: Espresso and Filter Couple
data.hero_headline: One loves espresso. One loves filter. Both are right.
data.hero_subtext: The setup that keeps the peace and satisfies both palates.
data.editorial_intro: You drink espresso — short, intense, concentrated. Your partner drinks filter — long, smooth, meditative. You have tried each other's preference and found it wanting. This is not a problem to be solved. It is a difference to be equipped for. Two mach
data.excerpt: The setup that keeps the peace and satisfies both palates.

data.title: Flatmates Shared Machine
data.hero_headline: Three people. One machine. Zero drama.
data.hero_subtext: The shared coffee setup that actually works, because someone finally chose the right equipment.
data.editorial_intro: Living with other people means compromising on the thermostat, the bathroom schedule, and what counts as a clean kitchen. Coffee should not be another battleground. But when three adults with three different skill levels and three different ideas abo
data.excerpt: The shared coffee setup that actually works, because someone finally chose the right equipment.

data.title: Home Coffee Tasting
data.hero_headline: Six origins. Four friends. One afternoon that changes how everyone tastes coffee.
data.hero_subtext: You do not need to be a professional to host a cupping. You just need good beans and curious people.
data.editorial_intro: The idea started as a casual suggestion over dinner — 'We should do a coffee tasting sometime.' Two weeks later, you have six single-origin beans, a set of cupping bowls, a freshly printed tasting wheel, and four friends arriving at three o'clock. Yo
data.excerpt: You do not need to be a professional to host a cupping.

data.title: Impressing the In-Laws
data.hero_headline: Great espresso when the stakes feel high.
data.hero_subtext: They are judging the coffee. You know it. The machine does not care.
data.editorial_intro: Your partner's parents are coming for lunch. They are Italian, or they lived in Melbourne for a decade, or they simply have opinions about coffee that they express with the directness of people who have been drinking espresso since before you were bo
data.excerpt: They are judging the coffee.

data.title: The Home Cafe
data.hero_headline: The best cafe in the neighbourhood is your kitchen.
data.hero_subtext: When friends stop suggesting going out for coffee and start suggesting coming to yours.
data.editorial_intro: There was no sign. No grand opening. Nobody applied for a licence or designed a logo. It just happened gradually — friends started coming over for coffee instead of meeting at the cafe down the road. Your kitchen became the place. The coffee was bett
data.excerpt: When friends stop suggesting going out for coffee and start suggesting coming to yours.
